qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Default DATAGRAM receive buffering allows count-based memory amplification DoS
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The finding is in the same `quinn-proto` package but it targets DATAGRAM queue accounting in `DatagramState::received` within `quinn-proto/src/connection/datagrams.rs`, not the advisory's `TransportParameters::read` varint `unwrap()` panic in `quinn-proto/src/transport_parameters.rs`.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No qualifying finding identifies the advisory's malformed `quic_transport_parameters` parsing bug in `TransportParameters::read()` where attacker-controlled varints were decoded with `unwrap()` and could panic on truncated input.
evidence: The advisory summary and PR #2559 isolate the root cause to `quinn-proto/src/transport_parameters.rs`, where `MaxDatagramFrameSize` and `MinAckDelayDraft07` used `r.get().unwrap()` on attacker-controlled transport-parameter varints; the fix replaces those calls with `r.get()?`.

At the vulnerable commit used by the session finding, `quinn-proto/src/transport_parameters.rs` still contains `params.max_datagram_frame_size = Some(r.get().unwrap());`, which matches the advisory's panic-on-truncated-varint parser path.

The only session-materialized qualifying finding instead targets `quinn-proto/src/connection/datagrams.rs:132-139`, where `DatagramState::received` enforces a byte window but still queues one `Datagram` object per frame, leading to count-based memory amplification after a successful QUIC handshake.

That finding requires many 1-byte DATAGRAM frames on an established connection and does not identify malformed transport-parameter parsing, `UnexpectedEnd`, the `TransportParameters::read` parser state, or the advisory's single-packet panic behavior.
